title: Breadth-First Search
code: static int[] BfsShortest(int n, Dictionary<int, List<int>> adj, int src)

frames.caption: Start at source 0: mark it visited and enqueue it. (Mark visited on enqueue, not on dequeue, so no vertex enters the queue twice.)
frames.note: BFS explores in layers — all vertices at distance 1, then all at distance 2, and so on — using a FIFO queue as the frontier. The golden rule is to mark a vertex visited the moment you ENQUEUE it. Marking on dequeue instead would let the same vertex enter the queue several times, bloating it and breaking the distance guarantee.

frames.caption: Dequeue 0 and make it the current vertex. Scan its neighbors: [1, 2].
frames.note: Take the vertex at the FRONT of the queue — by FIFO order it is the closest unprocessed vertex to the source. We expand it by examining each neighbor. Because the queue always drains nearer vertices before farther ones, vertices are processed in nondecreasing distance order, which is what gives BFS its shortest-path guarantee.

frames.caption: Neighbor 1 is unvisited → mark it visited and enqueue it (its BFS distance is now fixed). Queue: [1].
frames.note: This neighbor has not been seen, so the FIRST time we reach it is necessarily along a shortest path (every edge has weight 1). We fix its distance now and add it to the back of the queue. Marking it visited at this moment is exactly what stops it from ever being enqueued a second time.

frames.caption: Neighbor 2 is unvisited → mark it visited and enqueue it (its BFS distance is now fixed). Queue: [1, 2].
frames.note: This neighbor has not been seen, so the FIRST time we reach it is necessarily along a shortest path (every edge has weight 1). We fix its distance now and add it to the back of the queue. Marking it visited at this moment is exactly what stops it from ever being enqueued a second time.

frames.caption: All of 0's neighbors are handled, so 0 is fully visited.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex has been examined, so it is fully processed and will never be touched again. Across the whole run each vertex is enqueued once and each edge inspected once, which is why BFS runs in O(V + E) — efficient even on large sparse graphs.

frames.caption: Dequeue 1 and make it the current vertex. Scan its neighbors: [0, 2].
frames.note: Take the vertex at the FRONT of the queue — by FIFO order it is the closest unprocessed vertex to the source. We expand it by examining each neighbor. Because the queue always drains nearer vertices before farther ones, vertices are processed in nondecreasing distance order, which is what gives BFS its shortest-path guarantee.

frames.note: Take the vertex at the FRONT of the queue — by FIFO order it is the closest unprocessed vertex to the source. We expand it by examining each neighbor. Because the queue always drains nearer vertices before farther ones, vertices are processed in nondecreasing distance order, which is what gives BFS its shortest-path guarantee.

We fix its distance now and add it to the back of the queue. Marking it visited at this moment is exactly what stops it from ever being enqueued a second time.

frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex has been examined, so it is fully processed and will never be touched again. Across the whole run each vertex is enqueued once and each edge inspected once, which is why BFS runs in O(V + E) — efficient even on large sparse graphs.

frames.caption: Dequeue 3 and make it the current vertex. Scan its neighbors: [2, 4].
frames.note: Take the vertex at the FRONT of the queue — by FIFO order it is the closest unprocessed vertex to the source. We expand it by examining each neighbor. Because the queue always drains nearer vertices before farther ones, vertices are processed in nondecreasing distance order, which is what gives BFS its shortest-path guarantee.

frames.note: This neighbor has not been seen, so the FIRST time we reach it is necessarily along a shortest path (every edge has weight 1). We fix its distance now and add it to the back of the queue. Marking it visited at this moment is exactly what stops it from ever being enqueued a second time.

frames.caption: All of 3's neighbors are handled, so 3 is fully visited.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex has been examined, so it is fully processed and will never be touched again. Across the whole run each vertex is enqueued once and each edge inspected once, which is why BFS runs in O(V + E) — efficient even on large sparse graphs.

frames.caption: Dequeue 4 and make it the current vertex. Scan its neighbors: [3].
frames.note: Take the vertex at the FRONT of the queue — by FIFO order it is the closest unprocessed vertex to the source. We expand it by examining each neighbor. Because the queue always drains nearer vertices before farther ones, vertices are processed in nondecreasing distance order, which is what gives BFS its shortest-path guarantee.

frames.caption: All of 4's neighbors are handled, so 4 is fully visited.
frames.note: Every neighbor of this vertex has been examined, so it is fully processed and will never be touched again. Across the whole run each vertex is enqueued once and each edge inspected once, which is why BFS runs in O(V + E) — efficient even on large sparse graphs.

frames.caption: Queue is empty — BFS is complete. Vertices came out in nondecreasing distance order: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
frames.note: Every reachable vertex has been visited. The order they came out is nondecreasing in distance from the source, which is precisely why BFS on an unweighted graph yields shortest paths. The instant edges carry different weights, this guarantee breaks and you need Dijkstra instead.
